site.title: Fundamental of X — Visual, Interactive Deep-Dives
site.footer: Built with curiosity. Made for learners who think with pictures.
site.topicFooter: Part of the Fundamental of X series.

shared.mascotIntro:
Hi! I'm X-sensei.
I'll point at the important bits while you learn.

support.cardBody: Buy X-sensei a coffee and keep the site ad-free.

hero.title:
Learn the foundations,
one X at a time.
hero.body: A visual, interactive companion for foundational topics that are usually explained with walls of text. Every lesson is hands-on — you click, drag, and watch the concept happen instead of just reading about it.

about.heading: Why visual?
about.p1: A picture of a B-Tree splitting is worth more than three paragraphs of prose, and an animation of a transaction rolling back beats any bullet list. This site is built for learners who want to see ideas move, not just read about them.
about.p2: Each subject is a self-contained module.

compilers.title: Compilers
compilers.tagline: How source code becomes something a CPU can run.
compilers.metaTitle: Fundamental of Compilers — Visual Crash Course
compilers.intro: Eight passes between the file you wrote and the bytes the CPU runs. Lexer, parser, AST, type checker, IR, optimizer, register allocator, code generator — each step throws information away on purpose, and each step has a widget you can poke at.

card.title: Fundamental of Compilers
card.body: Lexer, parser, AST, type checker, IR, optimizer, register allocation, code generation — eight passes between the file you wrote and the bytes the CPU runs.

lexer.title: Lexer & Tokens
lexer.blurb: A stream of characters becomes a stream of tokens. The first transformation: text → atoms with meaning.

parser.title: Parsing & Grammar
parser.blurb: Tokens get shape. Precedence, associativity, and how a grammar turns "1+2*3" into a tree.

ast.title: Abstract Syntax Tree
ast.blurb: Strip the syntax, keep the meaning. The shape every later pass actually walks.

types.title: Type Checking
types.blurb: Walk the tree, decorate it with types, reject the programs that lie. Inference vs annotation.

ir.title: IR & SSA Form
ir.blurb: High-level trees become low-level instructions. Every variable assigned exactly once.

optimization.title: Optimization Passes
optimization.blurb: Constant folding, dead code, common subexpressions. Each pass throws code away faster than you write it.

regalloc.title: Register Allocation
regalloc.blurb: Infinite virtual registers → 16 real ones. Live ranges, interference graphs, graph coloring.

codegen.title: Code Generation
codegen.blurb: IR becomes x86 / ARM / RISC-V. Instruction selection, calling conventions, the final emit.

lexer.title: Lexer & Tokens
lexer.blurb: Source code is a flat string. The first job of a compiler is to chop it into tokens — atomic units like let, x, =, 42 — and tag each one with what kind of thing it is. Whitespace and comments get thrown away.
lexer.section1Body: It scans left-to-right with a tiny state machine, recognising patterns: a letter starts an identifier or keyword, a digit starts a number, a quote starts a string. Each match becomes a token. The lexer doesn't care what the program means yet — only what shapes the characters form.

takeaways.t1: The lexer's output is a flat sequence — no structure yet, just labelled atoms.
takeaways.t2: Whitespace and comments are usually discarded here. Position info is kept for error messages.
takeaways.t3: Most real lexers are generated from regex-like rules (flex, ANTLR) or hand-written for speed.
takeaways.t4: Keywords are just identifiers that happen to be reserved — the lexer treats them specially.

parser.title: Parsing & Grammar
parser.blurb: Tokens have no structure — just a flat list. The parser takes that list and discovers the tree hidden inside, guided by a grammar. Operator precedence and associativity decide which tree.
